sections.title: Regulation evaluation timeline and scope
sections.summary: Four years after the Regulation's entry into force, the Commission must evaluate the Regulation and submit a report to the European Parliament, Council, and European Economic and Social Committee. The evaluation assesses data accessibility categories, excluded data categories, contractual practices, financial data sharing schemes, and compensation impacts on data users.

sections.heading: RESULTS
sections.title: Revocation of Commission delegation authority
sections.summary: The European Parliament or Council may revoke the Commission's delegation of powers at any time. Revocation takes effect the day after publication in the Official Journal and does not affect the validity of previously enacted delegated acts.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a mechanism for EU institutions to terminate delegated authority over financial data transfers, ensuring accountability and oversight of cross-border financial data activities.

requirements.text: European Parliament or Council may revoke Commission's delegation of powers at any time
Revocation takes effect the day following publication in the Official Journal of the European Union
Revocation does not affect validity of delegated acts already in force

sections.source_quote: The delegation of powers referred to in Article 11, may be revoked at any time by the European Parliament or by the Council. A decision to revoke shall put an end to the delegation of the power specified in that decision. It shall take effect the day following the publication of the decision in the Official Journal of the European Union or at a later date specified therein. It shall not affect the validity of any delegated acts already in force.

sections.heading: RESULTS (cont.)
sections.title: Financial data access economic impact assessment
sections.summary: This section provides a macroeconomic assessment of the potential economic benefits and costs of enhanced financial data access in the EU. It states that the proposal could generate annual benefits between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion for the EU economy, including EUR 663 million to EUR 2 billion for the financial data economy. The proposal also involves one-off costs up to EUR 2.2 billion and recurring annual costs between EUR 147 million and EUR 465 million. The assessment notes that while the proposal supports sustainable finance and inclusive economic growth, its economic impact is difficult to quantify precisely due to data limitations and policy interdependencies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section describes potential economic benefits and costs of financial data access but does not specify technical safeguards or cross-border transfer conditions relevant to DTIAs. It is not directly applicable to data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: Total annual benefits for EU economy range between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion
Direct impact on EU financial data economy ranges between EUR 663 million and EUR 2 billion annually
One-off costs could be up to EUR 2.2 billion
Recurring annual costs between EUR 147 million and EUR 465 million

sections.source_quote: According to this macroeconomic assessment, the total annual benefits for the EU economy produced by enhanced access to and sharing of data in the EU financial sector ranges between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion, including the direct impact on the EU financial data economy in the range of EUR 663 million to EUR 2 billion per year.

sections.heading: BUDGETARY IMPLICATIONS
sections.title: Consultation of experts before adopting delegated acts
sections.summary: Before adopting a delegated act, the European Commission must consult experts designated by each Member State in accordance with the Interinstitutional Agreement of 13 April 2016 on Better Law-Making. This requirement ensures that Member State expertise is integrated into the legislative process prior to final adoption of delegated acts.

requirements.text: Before adopting a delegated act, the Commission shall consult experts designated by each Member State

sections.source_quote: Before adopting a delegated act, the Commission shall consult experts signated by each Member State in accordance with the principles laid down in the Interinstitutional Agreement of 13 April 2016 on Better Law-Making.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS
sections.title: Cooperation with supervisory authorities for personal data processing
sections.summary: When this Regulation's obligations involve processing personal data, competent authorities must cooperate with supervisory authorities established under GDPR. This ensures compliance with data protection requirements when handling personal information across borders.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates cooperation with GDPR supervisory authorities for personal data processing, which impacts DTIAs by requiring alignment with data protection standards during cross-border financial data exchanges.

requirements.text: Competent authorities must cooperate with GDPR supervisory authorities

sections.source_quote: Where obligations under this Regulation concern the processing of personal data, competent authorities shall cooperate with the supervisory authorities established pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Customer data sharing for financial innovation and trust
sections.summary: This section outlines the need for a regulatory framework enabling financial institutions to share customer data beyond payment accounts while ensuring customer control. It emphasizes that current data sharing is limited by technical barriers and legal obligations, restricting innovation and personalized financial services. The framework must balance data sharing with customer rights and privacy protections to foster trust and innovation in the financial sector.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes that customer data sharing beyond payment accounts requires explicit customer permission and must respect data protection principles. It sets conditions for data sharing that impact cross-border transfers by mandating lawful bases and customer consent, relevant for DTIAs involving financial data.

requirements.text: Customer data sharing beyond payment accounts must be based on customer permission
Data users must have a valid lawful basis for processing personal data
Data processing must respect principles of personal data protection including lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, and data minimisation
Customers have the right to withdraw permission granted to data users
Data sharing should not cover data related to sickness and health insurance or life insurance products
Data sharing should not cover creditworthiness assessment data
Data sharing must respect protection of confidential business data and trade secrets

sections.source_quote: Ensuring customer control and trust is imperative to build a well-functioning and effective data sharing framework in the financial sector. Ensuring effective customers' control over data sharing contributes to innovation as well as customer confidence and trust in data sharing.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Financial data sharing for customer access and consent management
sections.summary: This section establishes that financial institutions must provide defined categories of customer data upon request, allowing sharing based on customer consent. It requires data holders to implement permission dashboards that give customers control over data sharing, including real-time updates on permissions and withdrawal risks. The dashboards must be accessible, impartial, and comply with accessibility standards, with options to use electronic identification services or data intermediation providers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to implement permission dashboards for customer data access management, ensuring transparency and control over data sharing. This impacts cross-border data transfers by mandating standardized interfaces and consent mechanisms that must be consistent across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must provide defined categories of customer data at request
Data holders must implement permission dashboards for customers to manage data sharing
Permission dashboards must display active permissions and warn of contractual risks upon withdrawal
Data holders must inform data users in real-time of permission withdrawals
Permission dashboards must include records of withdrawn or expired permissions for up to two years
Data users must inform data holders in real-time of new or re-established permissions
Permission dashboards must be accessible, impartial, and comply with accessibility standards
Data holders may use electronic identification services or data intermediation providers to implement dashboards

sections.source_quote: Customers must have effective control over their data and confidence in managing permissions they have granted in accordance with this Regulation.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Financial data sharing schemes for cross-border data access
sections.summary: This section mandates the creation of financial data sharing schemes that establish standardized contractual frameworks, data interfaces, and governance rules for cross-border data sharing between financial institutions. These schemes must ensure proportionality, market competition, and consumer protection while complying with Union rules on data protection and competition. They require data holders and users to follow specific liability rules for data breaches and disputes.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires standardized financial data sharing schemes with contractual frameworks and governance rules to facilitate cross-border data access while ensuring proportionality and consumer protection. This directly impacts DTIAs by mandating specific technical and contractual safeguards for international data transfers.

requirements.text: Data holders and users must join financial data sharing schemes for contractual and technical interaction
Schemes must develop data and interface standards, joint contractual frameworks, and governance rules
Schemes must ensure inclusive governance with balanced representation of data holders, users, and customers
Schemes must have transparency requirements and effective appeal procedures for decisions
Schemes must comply with Union rules on consumer protection, data protection, privacy, and competition
Schemes must not restrict competition in a substantial part of the relevant market
Data holders may use existing market standards when developing common standards
Data holders may request reasonable compensation from data users for interface implementation
Compensation must be limited to costs incurred for facilitating data access for SMEs
Schemes must establish liability rules for data breaches and dispute resolution between data holders and users

sections.source_quote: Data holders and data users should be required to be part of financial data sharing schemes.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Periodic penalties and expedited enforcement for financial data breaches
sections.summary: Member States must establish specific rules for periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers and their management bodies. Competent authorities can impose these penalties to compel compliance with orders, such as providing information or terminating breaches. Penalties must be calculated daily unless Member States specify otherwise. Member States may encourage expedited enforcement procedures but are not required to implement them, and must notify the Commission if they do so.
sections.dtia_summary: Periodic penalties and expedited enforcement mechanisms create conditions for financial data breaches that require cross-border coordination and transparency in penalty applications. These mechanisms affect how penalties are calculated and enforced across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Member States should lay down specific rules and effective mechanisms regarding the application of periodic penalty payments
Competent authorities should be empowered to impose periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers
Periodic penalty payments should not prevent subsequent administrative sanctions for the same infringement
Periodic penalty payments should be calculated on a daily basis unless otherwise provided by Member States
Member States may encourage expedited enforcement procedures but are not required to implement them
Member States must notify the Commission if they empower competent authorities to use expedited enforcement procedures

sections.source_quote: Member States should lay down specific rules and effective mechanisms regarding the application of periodic penalty payments. Competent authorities should be empowered to impose periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers and on those members of their management body who are identified as responsible for an ongoing infringement or who are required to comply with an order from an investigating competent authority.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Data sharing and access for financial information service providers
sections.summary: Financial information service providers must notify their competent authority when accessing data held by data holders in other Member States. They must specify the type of data, financial data sharing scheme, and Member States where access is intended. This regulation ensures cross-border data access for financial services while maintaining regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial information service providers to notify authorities when accessing cross-border data, creating specific conditions for international data transfers that must be documented and reported.

requirements.text: Financial information service providers must notify their competent authority when accessing data held by data holders in another Member State
Providers must specify the type of data, financial data sharing scheme, and Member States where access is intended

sections.source_quote: A financial information service provider wishing to have access to data held by a data holder in another Member State, should notify its intention to its competent authority, providing information on the type of data it wishes to access, the financial data sharing scheme of which it is a member and the Member States in which it intends to access the data.

sections.title: Data sharing requirements for data holders
sections.summary: When sharing customer data with data users, data holders must provide data in standardized formats, ensure secure communication, verify customer authorization, provide permission dashboards for monitoring, and protect trade secrets and intellectual property rights. These requirements ensure secure and compliant data sharing practices.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies technical and procedural safeguards for data sharing, including format standards, security protocols, and customer authorization verification, critical for DTIA compliance in financial data transfers.

requirements.text: Make customer data available to the data user in a format based on generally recognised standards and at least in the same quality available to the data holder
Communicate securely with the data user by ensuring an appropriate level of security for the processing and transmission of customer data
Request data users to demonstrate that they have obtained the permission of the customer to access the customer data held by the data holder
Provide the customer with a permission dashboard to monitor and manage permissions in accordance with Article 8
Respect the confidentiality of trade secrets and intellectual property rights when customer data is accessed in accordance with Article 5(1)

sections.source_quote: When making data available pursuant to paragraph 1, the data holder shall: (a) make customer data available to the data user in a format based on generally recognised standards and at least in the same quality available to the data holder; (b) communicate securely with the data user by ensuring an appropriate level of security for the processing and transmission of customer data; (c) request data users to demonstrate that they have obtained the permission of the customer to access the customer data held by the data holder; (d) provide the customer with a permission dashboard to monitor and manage permissions in accordance with Article 8. (e) respect the confidentiality of trade secrets and intellectual property rights when customer data is accessed in accordance with Article 5(1).

sections.heading: The competent authority may withdraw an authorisation issued to a financial
sections.title: Conditions for withdrawing financial information service provider authorisation
sections.summary: The competent authority may withdraw authorization from a financial information service provider if the provider fails to use the authorization within 12 months, renounces it, ceases business for over six months, obtained authorization through false statements, no longer meets authorization conditions, fails to report major developments, or poses a risk to consumer protection and data security. The authority must provide reasons and notify affected parties, and publish the withdrawal anonymously.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines conditions under which a regulator can revoke authorization for financial data services, impacting cross-border data transfers by requiring ongoing compliance and timely reporting to ensure service providers meet security and consumer protection standards.

requirements.text: The provider does not use the authorization within 12 months
The provider expressly renounces the authorization
The provider has ceased to engage in business for more than 6 months
The authorization was obtained through false statements or irregular means
The provider no longer meets conditions for granting authorization
The provider fails to inform the competent authority on major developments
The withdrawal constitutes a risk to consumer protection and data security
The competent authority gives reasons for withdrawal
The competent authority informs those concerned of withdrawal
The competent authority publishes withdrawal in anonymized version

sections.source_quote: The competent authority may withdraw an authorisation issued to a financial information service provider only if the provider: (a) does not make use of the authorisation within 12 months, expressly renounces the authorisation or has ceased to engage in business for more than 6 months; (b) has obtained the authorisation through false statements or any other irregular means; (c) no longer meets the conditions for granting the authorisation or fails to inform the competent authority on major developments in this respect; (d) would constitute a risk to consumer protection and the security of data. The competent authority shall give reasons for any withdrawal of an authorisation and shall inform those concerned accordingly. The competent authority shall make public the withdrawal of an authorisation, in an anonymised version.

The report may be accompanied by a legislative proposal if deemed appropriate

sections.source_quote: By [OP please insert the date = 4 years after the date of entry into force of this Regulation, the Commission shall submit a report to the European Parliament and the Council assessing the conditions for access to financial data applicable to account information service providers under this Regulation and under Directive (EU) 2015/2366.
